title: Fixing Missing Right Child Node Handling in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, write a function to check if the tree is a valid binary search tree. A binary tree is a valid BST if it follows the property that all the nodes in the left subtree have a value less than their parent node and all nodes in the right subtree have a value greater than their parent node.
statement.inputFormat: A binary tree represented as a list of nodes where each node is a list with the node's value and its two children.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the tree is a valid binary search tree.
statement.notes: The time complexity of the function should be O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the tree.

examples.explanation: The given binary tree represents a valid binary search tree.

hints.text: Start by checking if the tree is empty.

editorial.title: Valid Binary Search Tree
editorial.intuition: To check if a binary tree is a valid BST, we can perform an in-order traversal of the tree and check if the nodes are in sorted order.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to solve this problem is to sort the nodes and check if they are in sorted order.
editorial.optimalSolution: Perform an in-order traversal of the tree and check if the nodes are in sorted order.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution has a time complexity of O(n log n) due to the sorting step.
commonMistakes: not checking if the tree is empty
not handling the case where the tree is a single node
